overview: This period is dominated by genuinely new clusters around LLM evaluation, safety, and control, with a clear shift from generic capability claims toward failure-mode analysis, controllability, and agent reliability. The fastest-consolidating directions are multimodal/world-model systems for robotics and interaction, plus memory/retrieval/control for LLM agents in coding and decision workflows. Another strong theme is specialization: research is narrowing toward task-specific neurons, position-aware recommendation, and domain-constrained forecasting rather than broad foundation-model claims. No major clusters are visibly shrinking in the top-ranked set; the more notable change is that several adjacent areas are simultaneously crystallizing into product-relevant subproblems that current shipping systems do not expose cleanly.

clusters.label: LLM controllability and evaluation
clusters.one_line: Benchmarks and diagnostics for constraint adherence, reasoning control, and reliability in multi-turn LLM use.
clusters.existing_landscape: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Microsoft all ship general LLM APIs with basic safety filters and limited eval tooling. Open-source evaluation stacks like lm-eval-harness, HELM, and promptfoo exist, but they mostly measure static task accuracy, not controllability under interactive constraints or drift across turns.
clusters.research_industry_gap: Research is now measuring whether an LLM follows explicit constraints, preserves intent over multiple turns, and prioritizes compliance over plausible-sounding completion. Shipping products still expose generic chat, coding, and agent endpoints without a first-class way to test or enforce these properties; the failure modes only show up after deployment. Papers like 'Models Recall What They Violate' and 'Compliance versus Sensibility' suggest the model can systematically drift toward sensibility even when users specify hard constraints, which is a different problem from ordinary hallucination.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build a control-plane for enterprise LLM deployments: a test harness and runtime policy layer that scores constraint adherence, detects multi-turn drift, and blocks unsafe or off-spec completions before they reach users. The first buyer is teams deploying internal copilots in regulated or high-stakes workflows: legal ops, clinical note drafting, software engineering, and customer support. Defensibility comes from owning the constraint ontology, turn-by-turn telemetry, and the labeled failure corpus that improves with every deployment. The first product should be an API gateway plus dashboard that tells a customer which prompts, agents, and model versions are violating which constraints, and can route low-confidence cases to human review.
clusters.why_now: This is a NEW cluster with 43 papers, the largest new direction in the set. The representative papers are not about raw benchmark scores; they are about measurable controllability failures, multi-turn utility recovery, and reliability-aware judging, which makes the operational gap obvious now.
clusters.risks: Incumbent model providers can add some of this into their own eval and safety suites, and generic observability vendors may extend into it. Also, many customers will underpay until a constraint violation causes a concrete incident, so sales may start as a compliance budget line rather than a core AI budget item.

clusters.label: Interactive retrieval and memory control
clusters.one_line: Benchmarks and controllers for when to retrieve, remember, abstain, or clarify in multi-turn LLM systems.
clusters.existing_landscape: LangChain, LlamaIndex, Pinecone, Weaviate, and Redis provide retrieval plumbing and memory stores. Agent frameworks such as OpenAI Assistants, Anthropic tool use, and various coding-agent stacks provide ad hoc retrieval and context injection, but not principled control over when retrieval helps versus harms.
clusters.research_industry_gap: The research focus has shifted from 'can the model retrieve?' to 'should it retrieve here, from which memory, and with what safety constraints?'. Papers like 'Learning When to Remember' and 'Detecting Clinical Discrepancies...' show that naive nearest-neighbor memory injection can create false positives, while interactive benchmarks like CarryOnBench expose utility loss from poor intent clarification. Current products mostly offer vector search and prompt stuffing; they do not manage abstention, clarification, or risk-sensitive memory retrieval as first-class decisions.
clusters.startup_thesis: Ship a memory policy engine for LLM agents that learns retrieval thresholds, chooses between clarify/answer/abstain, and logs evidence provenance for each decision. The first buyer is teams building coding agents, healthcare assistants, and enterprise knowledge assistants that need higher precision than generic RAG. Defensibility comes from domain-specific feedback loops: the system learns from false-positive memories, mis-clarifications, and user corrections in a way that simple vector databases cannot. Start with a plugin that sits between agent and memory store and automatically suppresses unsafe or low-value context injection.
clusters.why_now: This is a NEW 39-paper cluster with several papers specifically about risk-sensitive memory retrieval, adaptive reranking, and benchmark design for multi-turn utility recovery. The emergence of interactive benchmarks suggests the field has recognized that static retrieval metrics are insufficient.
clusters.risks: Vector database vendors and agent framework owners can add heuristic versions quickly, and many buyers may prefer to tune retrieval manually inside their existing stack. The harder risk is that the problem is highly workflow-specific, so a horizontal product may need strong verticalization to be compelling.

clusters.label: LLM attack surface and red-teaming
clusters.one_line: Multi-turn prompt injection, supply-chain poisoning, and activation-level defenses for LLM systems and agents.
clusters.existing_landscape: CrowdStrike, Palo Alto Networks, Microsoft Defender, and a growing set of AI-security startups cover adjacent model and app security. Prompt injection scanners and red-team tools exist in products from Lakera, Protect AI, HiddenLayer, and Prompt Security, but coverage is uneven for agentic and supply-chain attacks.
clusters.research_industry_gap: Research is moving from simple jailbreak detection to stateful, multi-turn attacks, model-code backdoors, and physical/system-level threat models for LLM-enabled robotics. The gap is that most shipping tools inspect prompts or outputs, while these papers show attacks can live in activations, training pipelines, local fine-tuning code, and cross-component workflows. 'Secret Stealing Attacks on Local LLM Fine-Tuning...' and 'Enhancing Linux Privilege Escalation Attack Capabilities...' show that open-weight and local deployment changes the threat model materially.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build an AI attack simulator and hardening suite for teams running private models, agents, or local fine-tuning pipelines. The product should continuously red-team prompt injection, memory corruption, supply-chain poisoning, and agent privilege escalation, then produce concrete mitigations and policy rules. Buyers are security teams at enterprises adopting open-weight models, plus vendors shipping agentic products. Defensibility comes from attack libraries, execution traces, and environment-specific exploit generation that improves against each target stack.
clusters.why_now: This cluster is NEW and already contains multiple distinct attack classes, including activation probing for multi-turn attacks, stateful jailbreak defense, and supply-chain code backdoors. That breadth signals a real market surface rather than a one-off paper trend.
clusters.risks: Big security vendors can bundle AI attack testing into existing platforms, and some of the most damaging exploits may be hard to reproduce in the wild. There is also a risk that the market stays evaluative rather than preventive unless enterprises face real incidents.

clusters.label: Unified multimodal world models for interaction
clusters.one_line: Joint modeling of text, motion, video, and action for human-object interaction, robotics, and real-time multimodal assistants.
clusters.existing_landscape: OpenAI, Google, and Meta all have multimodal model stacks; NVIDIA has robotics and simulation tooling; and OSS projects like Hugging Face, ROS, and Isaac Sim support adjacent workflows. But there is no major shipping product that unifies real-time multimodal interaction, action generation, and world-model prediction in one deployable system.
clusters.research_industry_gap: Research is pushing beyond separate perception and generation models toward shared latent spaces for text, video, motion, and robot action, including real-time full-duplex interaction. The industry gap is that current products are still split into chatbots, video generators, robot policies, and perception pipelines; they do not expose a single model that can observe, reason, and act across modalities with low latency. Papers like MiniCPM-o 4.5, MotuBrain, and ExoActor indicate the field is converging on interactive multimodal agents rather than static generation.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build a real-time multimodal control model for embodied systems: the first product would be a robotics and teleoperation stack that takes video, audio, and language, then outputs structured actions and dialogue with low-latency feedback. The first buyer is robotics labs and companies deploying home, warehouse, or service robots that need robust interaction under occlusion and partial observability. Defensibility comes from proprietary interaction data, synchronized sensor capture, and a closed loop between perception and action that generic model APIs cannot replicate. The commercial wedge is not general-purpose multimodal chat; it is a control layer for high-value embodied workflows.
clusters.why_now: This is a NEW cluster with 20 papers, including real-time full-duplex omni-modal interaction and unified driving world models. The representative work is no longer just about multimodal benchmarks; it is about deployable control and interaction architectures.
clusters.risks: Hardware and data collection are expensive, and most buyers will not need a full world model. Hyperscalers and robotics incumbents can also move quickly once the architecture hardens, so the startup needs a narrow initial deployment.

clusters.label: Tactile and deformable robot manipulation
clusters.one_line: Robotic learning for contact-rich manipulation using tactile sensing, deformable objects, and adaptive latent dynamics.
clusters.existing_landscape: Boston Dynamics, Tesla Optimus, Figure, and Agility Robotics cover general humanoid and mobile manipulation; Universal Robots, Franka, and Mujin cover industrial manipulation. Open-source and research stacks like ROS, Isaac Gym, and MoveIt exist, but there is no dominant shipping product for tactile-rich deformable-object manipulation or home-grade loco-manipulation.
clusters.research_industry_gap: The research is now solving contact-rich problems that standard vision-only policies fail on: deformable linear objects, tactile-aware loco-manipulation, and adaptive physical latent reasoning for VLA models. Current commercial systems still assume rigid objects, controlled environments, and sparse contact feedback, while these papers show the policy needs tactile state, latent dynamics, and more grounded action reasoning. That gap matters because the hardest real-world tasks are exactly the ones where vision is ambiguous and contact dynamics dominate.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build a manipulation stack for tasks where touch matters: cable handling, garment handling, kitchen prep, medical training rigs, and assisted home chores. The first product should be a tactile-enabled policy package plus hardware integration kit for robotics companies or advanced labs, with one or two narrow tasks that are repeatable and high-value. Defensibility comes from proprietary tactile datasets, calibrated sensors, and closed-loop policy learning on hard contact tasks. The buyer is not a general robotics platform customer; it is teams trying to make a specific manipulation task reliable enough for deployment.
clusters.why_now: This is NEW and already includes multiple papers on tactile-aware control, deformable objects, and action reasoning for VLA models, plus a survey indicating the data foundation is becoming explicit. The field is moving from vision-based demos to touch-aware policies for real contact-rich work.
clusters.risks: Hardware integration and data collection slow the sales cycle, and many tasks remain too variable for robust automation. If generalist foundation models improve faster than expected, some of the value may be absorbed into broader robotics stacks.

clusters.label: Physics-informed time series forecasting
clusters.one_line: Forecasting models that inject physical constraints, uncertainty estimates, and interpretable latent structure into domain time series.
clusters.existing_landscape: Nixtla, Amazon Forecast, Google Vertex AI forecasting, and Azure AutoML provide general forecasting tooling. In open source, GluonTS, Darts, PyTorch Forecasting, and neuralforecast are common, but they are mostly generic statistical/ML toolkits rather than physics-aware forecasting products.
clusters.research_industry_gap: Research is moving toward forecasts that respect domain dynamics: weather, CFD, energy, structural health, and irregular clinical series. Shipping products generally optimize point accuracy and convenience, but do not expose physically consistent models, uncertainty-calibrated latent structure, or interpretable constraints as a standard capability. Papers like PINN-Cast, CircuITS, and explainable load forecasting with TSFMs show the gap is not just performance; it is trustworthiness and domain consistency.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build a forecasting product for asset-intensive operators that need forecasts they can act on: utilities, industrial plants, weather-sensitive logistics, and health monitoring teams. The first product should be a domain-specific forecasting API that lets customers add physical constraints, uncertainty bands, and explanation artifacts tied to their covariates and sensor topology. Defensibility comes from domain calibration data, constraint libraries, and workflow integration where inaccurate or physically impossible forecasts are operationally costly. The initial wedge should be one vertical with clear physics, such as energy demand or industrial equipment monitoring.
clusters.why_now: This is a NEW cluster and includes several papers that combine physics-informed losses, uncertainty modeling, and interpretable forecasting in real domain settings. The common theme is that generic foundation forecasting is not enough once operators need constraint satisfaction.
clusters.risks: Generic cloud forecasting services already cover the lowest end of the market, and many customers may not pay for extra structure unless errors are very expensive. Domain-specific modeling can also fragment the market and slow productization.

clusters.label: Agentic visual generation and software automation
clusters.one_line: Visual generation, video aesthetics, and self-evolving software agents that move from static outputs toward workflow execution.
clusters.existing_landscape: OpenAI, Adobe, Runway, Pika, and Midjourney already cover image and video generation. GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Replit, Devin-like coding agents, and various workflow automation tools cover software assistance, but no major product unifies agentic visual generation with autonomous workflow adaptation.
clusters.research_industry_gap: Research is signaling that the next step is not merely generating images or clips, but systems that understand goals, evaluate aesthetics, and self-improve inside software workflows. Current products can generate media or write code, but they do not expose a loop where the system critiques outputs, updates itself, and adapts to long-horizon tasks. 'Self-Evolving Software Agents' and 'AesRM' are representative of a shift toward feedback-driven generation and autonomous improvement rather than one-shot creation.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build a creative-production agent for a specific workflow such as marketing video iteration or internal software documentation generation, where the model can generate, evaluate, and revise assets from a brief. The first buyer is a content or engineering team that spends time on repetitive generation plus review cycles. Defensibility comes from feedback data on what revisions humans accept, reject, and reuse, plus integration with the tools where content already lives. The startup should not sell generic generation; it should sell a closed-loop production assistant that improves over repeated jobs.
clusters.why_now: This cluster is NEW and combines visual generation, search disruption, and self-evolving software agents, showing that the research community is linking generative media to autonomous workflow execution. That linkage is materially different from last-generation image/video synthesis work.
clusters.risks: This space is crowded and incumbent-capitalized, especially in media generation. Many use cases may remain novelty-oriented unless the product lands on a workflow with measurable time savings.

clusters.label: Neuron-level LLM editing and circuit analysis
clusters.one_line: Methods for locating, pruning, probing, and editing specific neurons or feature subspaces inside language models.
clusters.existing_landscape: OpenAI, Anthropic, and other frontier labs do internal model editing and alignment work, but they do not expose a general product for neuron-level editing. Open-source interpretability tools like TransformerLens, CircuitsVis, and various pruning libraries exist, but they are research tools rather than production systems.
clusters.research_industry_gap: Research is becoming much more specific about where behaviors live in the network: task-specific neurons, FFN behavioral circuits, semantic feature geometry, and small subnets that control personality or bias. Industry tools mostly operate at the prompt, dataset, or fine-tuning level; they do not let customers surgically edit model behavior without retraining. That creates a gap for organizations that need to remove a behavior, inject a policy, or compress a task-specific model without losing performance.
clusters.startup_thesis: Build a model surgery tool for enterprises that host or fine-tune open-weight models: identify the neurons or circuits responsible for unwanted behaviors, then patch them with minimal performance loss. The first buyer is a team shipping domain models under cost or compliance constraints, such as support, legal, or coding. Defensibility comes from a behavior-to-circuit library accumulated across model families and tasks, plus tooling that validates edits against regression suites. The first product should be a narrow editor for safety/compliance or personality control on a few popular open-weight models.
clusters.why_now: This is a NEW cluster with multiple papers that go beyond interpretability into direct editing and compression, including personality neuron localization and pruning collapse/recovery. That suggests the community is looking for actionable circuit-level interventions rather than just explanations.
clusters.risks: Hyperscalers and model providers may keep the highest-value editing capabilities internal, and many customers will choose fine-tuning or prompt engineering over surgery tools. The technical risk is high because edits may not transfer cleanly across model families or updates.
